# Transcrição: When_Are_Ad_Results_Actually_Meaningful_

## [00:00 - 02:59]

This is the pubcast shot. I'm John Loomer So when are results meaningful? Let's go I'm the last episode I talked about letting results be your guide when it comes to making changes to your Advertising now when I get questions like how often should I update creative or should I restrict by age or gender? Or should I use look-alike audiences or go broad and on and on and on? Don't rely on advice that attempts to frame an approach based on universal truths Don't assume you should do one thing or another Let results be your guide your results that are unique to you if you're getting great results Don't mess with it. If you're no longer getting good results It's probably time to make a change But it's important we apply a caveat here when you let results be your guide and make changes based on them Those results need to actually be meaningful in the first place. Here's what I mean Number one the volume of results Needs to be meaningful I see this far too often an advertiser is running ads for a few days or a week at a modest budget Optimizing for a purchase they have separate ad sets for advantage plus audience and look like audiences They get 10 purchases using advantage plus audience in 15 from look like audiences They then declare that look like audiences work better than advantage plus audience and turn off one of the ad sets Now ignore for a moment the fact that the algorithm likely reached many of the same types of people in either case here The volume of results is not meaningful to learn anything definitive And if you're thinking that an ab split test would fix that I have an example for you. I once ran a split test of three identical ad sets That highlights this problem met and declared that one ad set was a clear winner Generating 25 percent more conversions than one of the other ad sets But since everything about the ad sets and the ads within them was identical We know these results were random. We couldn't say it was because of the targeting Or optimization or a copy or the creative But we make decisions like this based on 25 differences all the time We assume performance differences are caused by the differences in targeting or creative But that may not be the case. It could be correlation

## [03:00 - 06:02]

Not causation now at some point the number is meaningful But where does that happen instead of giving you a definitive number? I'd look at stability And predictability of your results from day to day and week to week Can your interpretation of results change based on a single day of results? So for example when we were talking about The situation where there were 10 and 15 conversions Based on ad set based on targeting a single day could completely transform your interpretation So make sure that volume of results is meaningful number two what you focus on needs to be meaningful some advertisers are overly focused on metrics like click-through rate cost per click CPM and honestly several others these metrics should not be your foundation for how you judge performance You shouldn't stop an ad set or choose an ad based on any of these secondary metrics While they help tell a story. They aren't your bottom line Your focus should be on the cost per action Which is hopefully a conversion of some kind and it might also be on return on ad spend But click-through rate for example isn't predictive of either of these metrics You can get a high ctr that doesn't lead to conversions You might also get a lower ctr that sends qualified traffic that is more likely to convert The same is true of focusing too much on cpm So focus on results based on meaningful metrics when making decisions number three the quality of results needs to be meaningful So let's assume you're comparing two ads when optimizing for leads We get meaningful volume to make a judgment of performance ad a generates 1000 leads and ad b Generates 800 while spending the same amount overall Since ad a generated more leads. Do we declare it the winner and turn off ad b? Not necessarily quality matters too Maybe ad a was more persuasive than ad b Which led to more leads but ad b did a better job of attracting the right people Which resulted in more qualified leads than ad a you could actually run into similar issues with ads optimized for purchase One could generate more sales at a lower cost per purchase But the other might generate a higher return on ad spend So more value per purchase So make sure that the quality not just the quantity of your results is also meaningful number four the Incrementality of results needs to be meaningful

## [06:02 - 09:02]

So sometimes our results can be misleading especially when running ads for a sale You have one ad set using remarketing with a crazy 20x return on ad spend and another Going broad that is 4x a bit more modest. Is the remarketing ad set more effective? Not necessarily Because incrementality is important in other words How many of your conversions wouldn't have happened if people hadn't seen your ad? Well, you can make the argument that your ads influence the conversion with remarketing There are times when this isn't the case Some of those view-through conversions are completely worthless and even if you focus on click-through conversions There are some cases where your ads may not have been required to get the conversion at all This isn't to say that remarketing makes no impact at all, but be very skeptical of your results Don't make decisions based on a surface level reporting because those numbers are not meaningful If you have incremental attribution, which is rolling out now Focus there or on one day click when comparing performance So here's the bottom of the glass. Here's what I want you to walk away with It's very easy to fall into the trap of making bad decisions Based on results that simply are not meaningful Make sure the volume of results is meaningful the metrics you make decisions on are meaningful The quality of results is meaningful and the incrementality is meaningful Truthfully, some or all of these things are rarely meaningful when advertisers make judgments about performance Especially the typical advertiser who doesn't spend enough money or creates too much complexity And then obsesses over things like copy and creative variations when the volume simply isn't there Focus on meaningful results Create less complexity to help generate meaningful results and don't obsess over the small stuff This simply doesn't mean much I have written well over a thousand blog posts over the years for johnlimmer.com on the topic of facebook now meta advertising And this includes anything from tutorials to new feature announcements To case studies and tests and experiments and a little bit of everything in between To be honest with you this my blog is what I became famous for it's why I have a business in the first place Now while not everybody consumes content in this way, especially a detailed tutorial, which is kind of my expertise Maybe you do and I hope you're following me there. I publish one blog post every week right now I've changed my cadence through the years, but that's where I'm following these days
